subject: History
chapter: Chapter: Weavers, Iron Smelters and Factory Owners

key_points: Indian weavers produced cotton and silk textiles that were highly demanded in Asia and Europe during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.
The East India Company gradually gained control over the textile trade and appointed agents called gomasthas to supervise weavers.
Weavers were given advances by the Company but were forced to sell their cloth only to the Company at low prices.
The Industrial Revolution in Britain introduced machines like the power loom, which increased textile production.
Machine-made cloth from Britain flooded Indian markets and reduced the demand for traditional handwoven textiles.
examples: Muslin from Bengal was one of the finest cotton textiles produced by Indian weavers.
Chintz and calico were popular Indian textiles exported to European markets.
Gomasthas often controlled weavers strictly and prevented them from selling cloth to other traders.
Textile mills began to develop in cities like Bombay during the late nineteenth century.
formulas: Traditional textile production → Spinning cotton → Weaving on looms → Finished cloth
Company control system → Advances → Supervision by gomasthas → Cloth supplied only to Company
Industrial change → Handloom production → Machine production → Growth of factories
exam_tips: Explain the role of gomasthas in controlling weavers.
Describe how the East India Company controlled textile production.
Understand the impact of the Industrial Revolution on Indian weaving industries.
Use examples of famous textiles such as muslin, chintz and calico.
